messages.content: Hey Marcus, thanks for jumping on the call. I've got Sarah from our solutions engineering team here as well.
Great, thanks for having us. Hi Sarah, good to meet you. I'm Tom, I lead procurement and vendor evaluation here at Centrix. I've got my colleague Dana on too — she's our head of IT infrastructure.

Perfect, Dana welcome. So Tom, why don't we start with a quick agenda check — we were thinking we'd do about 15 minutes on your current environment, then Sarah can walk through a technical overview, and we'll leave time for Q&A and next steps. Does that work?
Yeah that works for us. We've got a hard stop at the hour so that timeline sounds good.
Perfect. Before we dive in — how was your trip back from Chicago? I saw you mentioned you were at that conference last week.

Oh yeah, it was a long one. Flight got delayed so I didn't get home until almost midnight Thursday. But the conference itself was actually really valuable — lot of interesting conversations about where enterprise data management is heading.
Yeah I heard it was a good event. Did the topic of AI-driven automation come up much? We're seeing that dominate almost every conversation we have with prospects right now.
Constantly. Every session seemed to circle back to it. Honestly, that's part of why we started this evaluation — we feel like we're falling behind on that front with our current tooling.
That's a really common theme. Okay, so let's dig into that. Can you tell us a bit about your current environment? What are you working with today?

Sure. So we're running a pretty fragmented stack right now. We've got three separate data warehouses that don't talk to each other well, our reporting layer is mostly custom-built, and our ops team spends a significant amount of time on manual reconciliation every month. It's not sustainable at the scale we're growing.
How many data sources are we talking about feeding into those warehouses?
At last count, somewhere around 40 to 45 distinct sources. Mix of SaaS applications, some legacy on-prem systems, and a couple of third-party data feeds we license.
Got it. Sarah, do you want to jump in here?

Yeah absolutely. Tom, Dana — one thing I'm curious about is where the reconciliation bottleneck actually lives. Is it mostly at ingestion, at transformation, or is it more of a governance and lineage issue on the reporting side?

Honestly it's a little of all three, but if I had to rank them, transformation is the biggest pain point. Our schemas are inconsistent across sources and we don't have a standardized way to handle that.

That makes sense and it's very common at your scale. That's actually one of the core areas our platform addresses — we have an automated schema normalization engine that handles that mapping layer without requiring manual intervention every time a source schema changes.
Dana, does that resonate with what you're seeing on the infrastructure side?

Definitely. Every time one of our SaaS vendors pushes an update, we end up with broken pipelines and someone on my team has to drop everything to fix it. It's probably costing us 20 to 25 hours a month just in firefighting.
That's a significant hidden cost. If we can put a dollar figure on that engineer time, it usually tells a pretty compelling story for the ROI case. We can help you build that out.
Yeah that would actually be useful. Our CFO is going to want to see a concrete business case before we get to any approval stage.
Totally understood. We'll make sure the proposal reflects that. Sarah, do you want to start walking them through the platform overview?
Sure. I'm going to share my screen — just give me one second.
Take your time.

Okay so what you're looking at here is our unified data operations platform. The three main layers are ingestion, which handles your 40-plus sources, the transformation engine Tom mentioned, and then the governance and observability layer on top. Everything is managed through a single control plane.
How does the ingestion layer handle on-prem sources? That's been a sticking point with some of the other vendors we've looked at.

Good question. We use a lightweight on-prem agent that runs in your environment and handles the extraction without requiring any firewall changes or open inbound ports. It's outbound-only traffic, which most security teams are comfortable with.
Dana, that answers one of your concerns right?
It does. We had a whole back-and-forth with one of your competitors about that exact issue. They wanted us to open inbound access and our security team shot that down immediately.
Which competitor was that, if you don't mind me asking?
It was Velodata. We spent about six weeks going back and forth with them on the architecture before we decided to pause that evaluation.

Yeah we hear that a lot about Velodata. Their architecture made more sense when most infrastructure was on-prem but it hasn't evolved well for hybrid environments. We were designed with hybrid in mind from day one.
That does seem to be the difference. Are you also familiar with Nexstream? They're the other vendor we're actively looking at.

We are, yeah. They're a solid product, particularly for pure cloud environments. Where we tend to win in comparisons with them is on the governance and compliance side, and on total cost of ownership for complex source environments like yours.
Okay, that's helpful context. We do have some compliance requirements — we're in a regulated industry so audit trails and data lineage are important for us.
Sarah, can you pull up the governance module?

Already on it. So this is the lineage view — every transformation, every move of data is tracked automatically. You can trace any data point from its source all the way through to the final report, with timestamps and user attribution at each step.
That's actually exactly what our compliance team has been asking for. We had an audit last year where we couldn't produce lineage documentation fast enough and it was a painful experience.
This would have solved that completely. The audit export feature lets you generate a full lineage report for any dataset in a few clicks, formatted to meet most common regulatory frameworks.
Okay I have to say, this is checking a lot of boxes. Dana what are you thinking?
I'm impressed with the technical architecture. I do want to understand the deployment timeline and what lift is required from my team. We're stretched pretty thin right now.
That's a fair concern and one we take seriously. Sarah, do you want to speak to typical deployment timelines?

Of course. For an environment like yours — 40-plus sources, hybrid setup — a full production deployment typically runs 8 to 12 weeks. But we can get you to a working pilot with your top five to ten sources in as little as two to three weeks. And we provide a dedicated implementation engineer throughout the process so your team isn't carrying that alone.
A pilot actually sounds like the right starting point for us. I'd want to validate the schema normalization piece on our messiest data sources before we commit to a full rollout.
That's exactly the right way to approach it, and honestly it's how we recommend most customers start. A focused pilot lets you prove value quickly without a massive upfront commitment.
Yeah I think we want to proceed with a pilot program. Can we scope that out?

Absolutely. We'd define the source systems included, success criteria, and a timeline upfront so everyone's aligned on what done looks like. Marcus, do you want to talk through what that looks like from a commercial standpoint?

Sure. The pilot is typically structured as a fixed-fee engagement that rolls into the full contract if you decide to proceed. It gives you a clean decision point. I'll put together a full pricing proposal that covers both the pilot and the enterprise license options — I can have that to you by end of week.
End of week works. That gives us time to review before our internal steering committee meeting the following Tuesday.
Perfect. And on the sandbox side — Sarah, can we get their team access to a sandbox environment so Dana's team can start poking around the platform before the proposal lands?
Definitely. I'll set up a sandbox environment for them with some pre-loaded sample data that mirrors a multi-source hybrid setup. I can have that ready within 24 hours.
That would be great. Dana, that would give you and your team something concrete to evaluate before we get into contract conversations.
Yeah that's really helpful actually. I'd rather have my engineers kick the tires before we go too far down the commercial path.

Totally agree. It avoids surprises later. Tom, one thing I want to make sure we address — you mentioned a regulated industry. Are there specific compliance frameworks we should make sure we're covering in the proposal? SOC 2, HIPAA, something else?
SOC 2 Type II is the main one, and we also need to be GDPR compliant since we have European customers. If you have documentation on both of those I'd want to pass it to our legal team early.
We're certified on both. I'll include the compliance documentation pack in the proposal email so you have everything in one place.
Great, that'll save us a step.
One more thing on timeline — do you have a sense of where procurement is on their calendar? We want to make sure we're not running into any quarter-end crunches.

Actually that's worth flagging. Our procurement team has a pretty firm requirement to get any new vendor approvals through by end of Q2. After that there's a freeze until the new fiscal year budget is confirmed. So we're working with roughly a six-week window.

Okay, good to know. That's tight but workable if we move efficiently. If you get the proposal Tuesday, aim to have internal alignment by end of that week, we could potentially have a signed pilot agreement within two to three weeks, leaving buffer before the Q2 deadline.
That timeline works assuming legal doesn't throw any curveballs. We've had some contracts take longer than expected on the redline process.

We have a fairly lean standard agreement for the pilot specifically — it's designed to be easy to sign. The full enterprise contract is more involved but we don't need that until after the pilot anyway.
That's reassuring. Okay, I think we have a pretty clear path forward.

Agreed. Let me just recap the next steps so we're all aligned. Sarah is setting up the sandbox within 24 hours, I'm sending the pricing proposal by end of this week, your team reviews and we reconvene after your Tuesday steering committee meeting, and we're tracking against the end-of-Q2 procurement deadline. Does that capture it?
That covers it. Dana, anything to add on your end?
Nothing from me. I'll make sure my two senior engineers have time carved out to explore the sandbox this week.
Perfect. Sarah, I'll send you Dana's email directly so you can coordinate the sandbox access credentials.
Sounds good. Tom, Dana — any final questions before we let you go?
Just one. On the implementation engineer you mentioned — is that someone dedicated to us for the duration or more of a shared resource?

Great question. For pilot engagements it's a shared resource but with guaranteed response times. For the full enterprise deployment it's a dedicated resource for the duration of go-live. We can lay out both options in the proposal.
That works, yeah.
Excellent. Well this was a really productive call. You've given us a lot of good detail to work with and I think the pilot is a smart way to get started.
Agreed. I appreciate you both coming prepared — this was more substantive than most vendor calls we do, honestly.
That means a lot, thank you. Alright, we'll let you get back to it. Looking forward to sending that proposal over. Have a good rest of your week.
You too. Safe travels if you're on the road again anytime soon, Marcus.
Ha, I've got Chicago again in two weeks actually. The life of an AE. Take care everyone.
Ha, good luck. Thanks again. Bye.
